chunks.content:
| term | |
|---|---|
| [StableHLO](https://github.com/openxla/stablehlo) | the portable IR that JAX, PyTorch, and TensorFlow all emit. A dialect of MLIR defining ~100 machine learning operations with a 5-year stability guarantee. It's becoming the standard handoff point between ML frameworks and compilers. |
| [MLIR](https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/tree/main/mlir) | a compiler infrastructure for building reusable, composable passes. Programs are represented in dialects, each defining operations at a particular level of abstraction. Lets you progressively lower code from high-level ML ops down to hardware instructions. |
| [linalg dialect](https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/tree/main/mlir/include/mlir/Dialect/Linalg) | MLIR's dialect for structured linear algebra. Dense matmuls and convolutions live here. A common first stop when lowering ML ops toward hardware. |
| [GPU dialect](https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/tree/main/mlir/include/mlir/Dialect/GPU) | MLIR's dialect for GPU parallelism. Expresses thread blocks and memory hierarchies before targeting specific hardware. |
| [NVVM dialect](https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/tree/main/mlir/include/mlir/Dialect/LLVMIR) | MLIR's representation of NVIDIA's virtual machine IR. The last MLIR-level stop before PTX emission. |
| [PTX](https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/tree/main/llvm/lib/Target/NVPTX) | NVIDIA's low-level GPU assembly language. Human-readable but hardware-specific. What actually runs after the driver compiles it. |
| [LLVM NVPTX backend](https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/tree/main/llvm/lib/Target/NVPTX) | the LLVM backend that compiles LLVM IR to PTX for NVIDIA GPUs. Final step in the pipeline before the NVIDIA driver takes over. |
| [RTX 4080](https://github.com/NVIDIA/open-gpu-kernel-modules) | NVIDIA Ada Lovelace. 9,728 CUDA cores, 16GB GDDR6X. The hardware target for this series. |
| [JAX](https://github.com/jax-ml/jax) | Google's Python library for numerical computing. Supports automatic differentiation and JIT compilation via XLA. The frontend used to write and export the ops in this series. |
| [Transformer](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers) | a neural network architecture built around the attention mechanism. The backbone of virtually all modern large language models. |

content:
LLM services now answer billions of queries per day. OpenRouter alone served over [100 trillion tokens in 2025](https://andreyfradkin.com/assets/LLM_Demand_12_12_2025.pdf). Each request running the same operations - matmul and elementwise ops, over and over, on a GPU near you.
Pretraining an LLM is an expensive one-time cost, but it's not in the hot path. The compiler sitting between the framework and the hardware determines how fast, and by extension, how cheaply, model inference is.
This is a blog series on building a GPU compiler backend from scratch(ish). That _ish_ being Jax, StableHLO, MLIR, C++, and my trusty 4080.

JAX can already compile and run both of the operations we'll look at. So why build another compiler? Because "it runs" and "I understand why it runs" are different things. XLA is over a million lines of C++. IREE is hundreds of thousands. MLIR (in LLVM) is not far behind. I could [ask Claude Code to scaffold the whole thing](https://github.com/anthropics/claudes-c-compiler), let it run for a few days, then stare at the largest diff I've ever seen and try to reverse-engineer what happened. But that won't teach me how to build a compiler. This series is about building the minimum viable version: small enough to hold in your head, real enough to learn from.
But first, what are we actually compiling?
The operations we're compiling come from transformer inference. Getting these to run efficiently on a GPU is the compiler's job. Or a CPU, or TPU, or FPGA for that matter.

If you want a proper deep dive, [The Illustrated Transformer](https://jalammar.github.io/illustrated-transformer/) is one of the best resources on the internet. We're here for the compiler, so I'll keep this brief, but we do need an understanding of what we're handing to the compiler.

Transformers process text as sequences of tokens, each a word or subword mapped to a high-dimensional vector. A transformer is a stack of identical layers, each doing two things: mix information across all tokens ([attention](https://jalammar.github.io/illustrated-transformer/#self-attention-at-a-high-level)), then transform each token independently ([feed-forward](https://jalammar.github.io/illustrated-transformer/#now-were-encoding)). A large model repeats this block ~96 times.
Two operations do almost all the work:

- **Matrix multiply.** Attention projects each token into query, key, and value vectors via matmul. One matmul per projection, per layer.
- **Elementwise ops.** The feed-forward pass adds a learned bias and applies ReLU, one element at a time across the full tensor. The non-linearity that makes deep networks useful.

With Python at the top and bare metal at the bottom, a compiler sits in the middle. Its job: lower the human-understandable JAX down to GPU-understandable PTX. The code below exports two operations to [StableHLO](https://openxla.org/stablehlo) MLIR: a matrix multiply and an elementwise activation.

python.code:
import jax
import jax.numpy as jnp
from jax.export import export

def attention_projection(x, w):
    return jnp.matmul(x, w)

x = jax.ShapeDtypeStruct((1, 512, 768), jnp.float32)
w = jax.ShapeDtypeStruct((768, 768), jnp.float32)

exported = export(jax.jit(attention_projection))(x, w)
print(exported.mlir_module())

annotations.definition: Matrix multiply. Projects each of the 512 token embeddings through the weight matrix. Core op in Q, K, V attention projections.
annotations.why: One call in Python, one op in the output IR. Clean lowering.

annotations.definition: Just-In-Time compile. Traces the function and hands it to XLA. export() only works on jit-compiled functions.
annotations.why: JIT is the bridge between Python and the compiler. Without it JAX runs eagerly and there's nothing to export.

annotations.definition: A tensor's shape and dtype, no actual data. JAX traces the function symbolically using these. No real numbers needed.
annotations.why: Export needs static shapes upfront so the MLIR output can be fully typed at compile time.

annotations.definition: Batch 1, 512 tokens, 768 hidden dimensions. BERT-base hidden size.
annotations.why: 512 is the standard max sequence length for BERT-class models; 768 is the standard hidden dim.

annotations.definition: Square weight matrix. Input and output dimensionality are the same. The projection stays in the same embedding space.
annotations.why: In self-attention, Q/K/V projections preserve the embedding size. In, out, same.

annotations.definition: StableHLO's generalized dot product. Handles matmul, batched matmul, arbitrary tensor contractions.
annotations.why: jnp.matmul compiled to exactly one op. No broadcasting, no reshaping. Clean 1:1 lowering.

annotations.definition: Which dimensions to sum over: dim 2 of the first tensor (hidden) against dim 0 of the second (rows). The inner product of matrix multiplication.
annotations.why: Explicit dimension spec, nothing implicit. The compiler knows exactly what to fuse and parallelize.

annotations.definition: MLIR's static tensor type. Shape [1, 512, 768], element type float32.
annotations.why: MLIR is fully typed at compile time. Shape errors surface before any hardware code is generated.

content: The projection lowered cleanly. One Python call, one MLIR op.
The elementwise pass is more interesting.

python.code:
import jax
import jax.numpy as jnp
from jax.export import export

def elementwise(x, bias):
    return jax.nn.relu(x + bias)

x    = jax.ShapeDtypeStruct((1, 512, 768), jnp.float32)
bias = jax.ShapeDtypeStruct((768,), jnp.float32)

exported = export(jax.jit(elementwise))(x, bias)
print(exported.mlir_module())

annotations.definition: Rectified Linear Unit. f(x) = max(0, x). Clamps all negative activations to zero.
annotations.why: Introduces non-linearity after the bias add. Without it the whole layer is linear regardless of depth.

annotations.definition: Adds a per-dimension offset to every token embedding. The bias is 768-dim and JAX broadcasts it across all 512 tokens automatically.
annotations.why: Broadcasting is implicit in Python. In the MLIR output you can see what it actually costs: two ops before the add.

annotations.definition: One scalar bias per hidden dimension. No batch or sequence axis. Same bias for every token in every sequence.
annotations.why: Bias is positionally invariant. Each of the 768 hidden units gets its own offset, shared across the sequence.

mlir.code:
module @jit_elementwise {
  func.func public @main(
    %arg0: tensor<1x512x768xf32>,
    %arg1: tensor<768xf32>
  ) -> (tensor<1x512x768xf32>) {
    %0 = stablehlo.broadcast_in_dim %arg1,
           dims = [2] :
           (tensor<768xf32>)
        -> tensor<1x1x768xf32>
    %1 = stablehlo.broadcast_in_dim %0,
           dims = [0, 1, 2] :
           (tensor<1x1x768xf32>)
        -> tensor<1x512x768xf32>
    %2 = stablehlo.add %arg0, %1
         : tensor<1x512x768xf32>
    %3 = call @relu(%2)
         : (tensor<1x512x768xf32>)
        -> tensor<1x512x768xf32>
    return %3 : tensor<1x512x768xf32>
  }

  func.func private @relu(
    %arg0: tensor<1x512x768xf32>
  ) -> tensor<1x512x768xf32> {
    %cst = stablehlo.constant
             dense<0.000000e+00> : tensor<f32>
    %0 = stablehlo.broadcast_in_dim %cst,
           dims = [] :
           (tensor<f32>)
        -> tensor<1x512x768xf32>
    %1 = stablehlo.maximum %arg0, %0
         : tensor<1x512x768xf32>
    return %1 : tensor<1x512x768xf32>
  }
}

annotations.definition: Explicitly expands a tensor along new dimensions by repeating values. Turns the 768-dim bias into a 1x512x768 tensor.
annotations.why: Python's implicit broadcasting made explicit. What looked like one op is actually two broadcasts before the add.
The + in x + bias lands here, but only after both broadcast steps have materialized matching shapes.

annotations.definition: Elementwise max of two tensors. ReLU lowered as max(x, zeros_tensor).
annotations.why: relu is sugar in Python. In MLIR it's explicit: compare every value against 0, keep the larger one.

annotations.definition: JAX preserves function call boundaries when tracing. The Python relu call becomes its own private function in the module.
annotations.why: Function boundaries are a compilation hint. The compiler can choose to inline or keep it separate during optimization.

content: The philosophy of Python is that you can write a single, concise line of code to accomplish something powerful without worrying about the 5 triple kickflips it has to do under the hood to accomplish that something. The kickflips are abstracted away.

Comparatively, MLIR does not share this quality with Python. The projection is six lines. The elementwise pass is twenty-five. One Python expression (`relu(x + bias)`) became two broadcasts, an add, a constant, another broadcast, and a max.

This verbosity is the point - StableHLO makes every implicit operation explicit. Where in Python, broadcasting is invisible, in MLIR it costs two ops. Python's `relu` is sugar, but in MLIR it's `max(x, 0)`, spelled out explicitly. It also reveals an optimization opportunity: those five ops: broadcast, broadcast, add, broadcast, and max, each touch every one of the `1 × 512 × 768 = 393,216` values in the tensor independently. A naive backend launches five separate GPU kernels, but a smart compiler fuses them into one. That fusion pass is part of what this series builds.
The IR is in hand. Now the actual work begins.
